sections.name: Multiple Choice
sections.notes: Questions come in sets of 3-4 tied to a stimulus (primary text, secondary text, image, chart, or map). Require both stimulus analysis and broader historical knowledge.

sections.name: Short Answer
sections.notes: Q1 required (secondary source); Q2 required (primary source); Q3 or Q4 student choice (no stimulus). All four chronological periods represented.

sections.name: Document-Based Question
sections.notes: 7 documents offering multiple perspectives on one historical development. Includes 15-minute reading period. Use at least 6 of 7 documents.

sections.name: Long Essay
sections.notes: Select 1 from 3 prompts spanning different time periods. Tests argumentation, causation, comparison, or CCOT.

tips: MCQs are stimulus-based — read the source carefully before the question, then apply broader historical context.
For the DBQ, use at least 6 of 7 documents and include sourcing (HAPP: Historical context, Audience, Purpose, Point of view) for at least 3.
The LEQ and DBQ both reward a clear, defensible thesis with evidence and a reasoning process (causation, comparison, or CCOT).
SAQ responses should be concise — 3-5 sentences per part; no thesis required.
Practice cross-regional comparisons — the exam consistently tests your ability to connect developments across multiple regions.

units.name: The Global Tapestry
key_topics: Song Dynasty: Neo-Confucianism, civil service exam, Champa rice, Grand Canal
Dar al-Islam: Abbasid Caliphate fragmentation, Seljuk and Delhi Sultanates, Sufism, House of Wisdom
South and Southeast Asia: Vijayanagara Empire, Khmer Empire, Hinduism and Buddhism
State building in the Americas: Maya city-states, Mexica, Inca (chinampas, mita, waru-waru)
State building in Africa: Great Zimbabwe, Ethiopia, Hausa kingdoms
European feudalism: manorialism, serfdom, Catholic Church power
Great Schism (1054) and the Crusades
Syncretism and religious legitimation of political authority
Coercive labor systems: serfdom, mita, corvée
learning_objectives: Explain the systems of government employed by Chinese dynasties and how they developed over time.
Explain causes and effects of the rise of Islamic states and intellectual innovation in Dar al-Islam.
Explain how Hindu and Buddhist states of South and Southeast Asia developed and maintained power.
Explain how states in the Americas and Africa developed and changed.
Explain causes and consequences of political decentralization in Europe 1200-1450.
Explain similarities and differences in state formation across world regions 1200-1450.
common_question_types: Compare political systems across two or more world regions (e.g., Song bureaucracy vs. European feudalism).
Analyze how rulers used religion or ideology to legitimize authority.
Explain the spread of Islam or Buddhism across a region and its social effects.
Identify continuities and changes in coercive labor systems.
exam_tips: The exam favors cross-regional comparison in this unit — always connect Song China, Dar al-Islam, the Americas, and Europe.
Know specific examples of syncretism (rulers blending Hindu, Buddhist, and indigenous traditions to consolidate power).
Neo-Confucianism and the civil service exam are high-frequency MCQ topics.

units.name: Networks of Exchange
key_topics: Silk Roads: caravanserais, bills of exchange, paper money, luxury goods
Mongol Empire: Pax Mongolica, four khanates (Golden Horde, Il-Khanate, Yuan, Chagatai), trade facilitation
Indian Ocean trade: monsoon winds, compass, astrolabe, dhow and junk ships, Swahili city-states
Trans-Saharan trade: camel saddle, caravans, Mali Empire, Mansa Musa, Timbuktu
Travelers and documentation: Ibn Battuta, Marco Polo, Zheng He, Margery Kempe
Cultural consequences: spread of Buddhism, Islam, gunpowder, paper, Uyghur script
Environmental consequences: bubonic plague spread along trade routes
learning_objectives: Explain causes and effects of the growth of exchange networks after 1200.
Explain how the Mongol Empire influenced trade, cultural transfers, and state building.
Explain causes of Indian Ocean exchange growth and the role of environmental and technological factors.
Explain causes and effects of trans-Saharan trade and Mali's role in Afro-Eurasian networks.
Explain intellectual and cultural effects of connectivity across Afro-Eurasia.
Explain similarities and differences among the major exchange networks 1200-1450.
common_question_types: Analyze a primary source from a traveler (Ibn Battuta, Marco Polo) to identify trade network characteristics.
Explain how the Mongol Empire facilitated or disrupted exchange.
Compare the Silk Roads and Indian Ocean trade networks.
Explain environmental consequences of connectivity (bubonic plague).
exam_tips: The Black Death is a high-yield topic — know how it spread along trade routes and its demographic impact on Europe.
Mansa Musa's hajj is a frequent stimulus source; be ready to analyze evidence of Mali's wealth and its effect on Afro-Eurasian knowledge.
Pax Mongolica explains WHY long-distance trade expanded — connect Mongol political stability to economic exchange.

units.name: Land-Based Empires
key_topics: Gunpowder empires: Ottoman, Mughal, Safavid, Qing (Manchu)
Administrative systems: devshirme, janissaries, zamindars, tax farming, millet system, jizya
Legitimation through architecture: Taj Mahal, Versailles, Inca sun temples
Protestant Reformation: Martin Luther, 95 Theses, John Calvin, Counter-Reformation, Jesuits
Sunni-Shia split: Safavid-Mughal and Ottoman-Safavid conflicts
Sikhism as a new belief system
Japan: Tokugawa shogunate, sakoku (isolationism), daimyo, salaried samurai
Absolute monarchy and divine right in Europe
Thirty Years War (1618-1648)
learning_objectives: Explain how and why land-based empires developed and expanded 1450-1750 using gunpowder technology.
Explain how rulers legitimized and consolidated power through administrative, religious, and architectural means.
Explain continuity and change within belief systems 1450-1750.
Compare methods by which various empires increased influence across this period.
common_question_types: Compare administrative methods across two gunpowder empires (e.g., Ottoman devshirme vs. Mughal zamindars).
Analyze how monumental architecture served as a tool of political legitimation.
Explain the causes and consequences of the Protestant Reformation on European political unity.
Identify similarities and differences in how empires used religion to consolidate authority.
exam_tips: Know the four gunpowder empires and their specific administrative tools — these appear frequently as comparison prompts.
The Protestant Reformation disrupted European unity; connect it to later religious wars and political changes.
Distinguish between the Ottoman millet system (religious autonomy) and the Mughal jizya (tax on non-Muslims).

units.name: Transoceanic Interconnections
key_topics: Maritime technology: caravel, carrack, fluyt, astrolabe, magnetic compass
Key explorers: Columbus, Magellan, Vasco da Gama, Henry the Navigator
Columbian Exchange: transfer of crops, animals, diseases; population collapse in Americas
The Great Dying: 80-95% indigenous population loss from disease
Atlantic slave trade and chattel slavery; Triangular Trade
European maritime empires: Portuguese trading post empire, Spanish encomienda/hacienda, VOC, British East India Company
Colonial social hierarchies: casta system, peninsulares, creoles, mestizo, mulatto
Joint-stock companies and mercantilism
Responses: Ming isolationism, Tokugawa sakoku, Asante, Kingdom of Kongo
learning_objectives: Explain how cross-cultural interactions resulted in technological diffusion 1450-1750.
Explain economic causes and effects of maritime exploration and state-sponsored voyages.
Explain causes of the Columbian Exchange and its effects on Eastern and Western hemispheres.
Explain state building and expansion among maritime empires and how they established economic dominance.
Explain continuities and changes in systems of slavery and coercive labor.
Explain how social hierarchies changed as a result of transoceanic connections.
common_question_types: Analyze the demographic effects of the Columbian Exchange on indigenous American populations.
Explain how joint-stock companies enabled European colonial expansion.
Compare colonial labor systems (encomienda, chattel slavery, indentured servitude).
Contrast European expansionism with Ming and Tokugawa isolationist policies.
exam_tips: The Columbian Exchange is one of the highest-frequency topics on the exam — know specific crops, animals, and diseases that transferred and their effects.
Distinguish between the casta system labels: peninsulares (Spain-born) > creoles (Americas-born European) > mestizo (mixed) > mulatto (mixed African).
Joint-stock companies reduced financial risk for investors, enabling expensive overseas ventures.

units.name: Revolutions
key_topics: Enlightenment: natural rights, social contract, liberalism, nationalism
Atlantic Revolutions: American (1776), French (1789, Declaration of Rights of Man), Haitian (1791-1804), Latin American independence
Key figures: Toussaint L'Ouverture, Simon Bolivar, Mary Wollstonecraft
Industrial Revolution: factory system, steam engine, coal, enclosure movement, cottage industry
Second Industrial Revolution: railroads, telegraph, steamships
Economic ideologies: laissez-faire (Adam Smith), capitalism, socialism, communism (Karl Marx)
Labor responses: labor unions, socialism, communism
Government-led industrialization: Meiji Restoration, Muhammad Ali's Egypt, Self-Strengthening Movement
Social change: urbanization, new middle class, industrial working class, gender roles
learning_objectives: Explain the intellectual and ideological context for Atlantic revolutions 1750-1900.
Explain how Enlightenment ideas affected societies — suffrage, abolition, feminism, end of serfdom.
Explain causes and effects of various revolutions and nationalist movements 1750-1900.
Explain how technology and environmental factors shaped the Industrial Revolution.
Explain development of competing economic systems and ideologies.
Explain how industrialization changed social hierarchies and class structures.
common_question_types: Compare causes or outcomes of two Atlantic revolutions (e.g., French vs. Haitian).
Analyze how Enlightenment ideas influenced a specific political document or revolution.
Explain why industrialization began in Britain and how it spread.
Compare government responses to industrialization (Meiji Japan vs. Muhammad Ali's Egypt vs. laissez-faire Britain).
exam_tips: The Haitian Revolution is unique as the only successful slave revolution — emphasize this when comparing Atlantic revolutions.
Know the distinction between capitalism (Adam Smith), socialism, and Marxist communism for MCQs.
Meiji Japan and Muhammad Ali's Egypt both represent non-Western state-directed industrialization — compare them for LEQ prompts.

units.name: Consequences of Industrialization
key_topics: Ideological justifications for imperialism: Social Darwinism, White Man's Burden, civilizing mission, nationalism, religious conversion
Scramble for Africa: Berlin Conference (1884-85), settler vs. exploitation colonies
Economic imperialism: Opium Wars, Treaty of Nanjing, spheres of influence in China
Belgian Congo under King Leopold II
Indigenous responses: Indian Revolt of 1857, Boxer Rebellion, Taiping Rebellion
Migration patterns: indentured servitude, ethnic enclaves
Restrictive immigration: White Australia Policy, Chinese Exclusion Act
Shift from company to state colonial control (British East India Company to Crown rule)
learning_objectives: Explain how ideologies contributed to the expansion of imperialism 1750-1900.
Explain how states expanded through settler colonies and economic imperialism.
Explain how imperialism affected existing social structures in colonized regions.
Explain causes and effects of migration patterns during the Age of Imperialism.
Explain indigenous responses to state expansion and foreign domination.
common_question_types: Analyze a primary source justifying imperialism (Social Darwinism or civilizing mission rhetoric).
Explain how the Berlin Conference formalized European imperialism in Africa.
Compare settler colonies with exploitation colonies.
Explain effects of economic imperialism using the Opium Wars as a case study.
exam_tips: Social Darwinism applied biological 'survival of the fittest' to nations — be ready to analyze pro-imperialist sources using this framework.
The Berlin Conference is significant because European powers divided Africa without African representation or consent.
Distinguish between economic imperialism (informal control via trade/debt) and direct colonial rule.

units.name: Global Conflict
key_topics: Causes of WWI: militarism, alliance system, imperialism, nationalism (MAIN)
Conducting WWI: total war, trench warfare, propaganda, home front mobilization
Treaty of Versailles: reparations, war guilt, mandate system, League of Nations
Interwar crises: Great Depression, Five Year Plans (USSR), New Deal (US), rise of fascism
Causes of WWII: Treaty of Versailles grievances, fascism, Hitler's expansionism
Conducting WWII: totalitarianism, atomic bomb, fire bombing, genocide
Mass atrocities: Holocaust, Armenian Genocide, Rwandan Genocide, Cambodian genocide
State collapses leading to WWI: Ottoman, Russian, Qing empires
Russian Revolution and Bolshevik seizure of power (1917)
learning_objectives: Explain how internal and external factors contributed to state change after 1900.
Explain causes and consequences of World War I.
Explain how governments mobilized populations and resources for total war.
Explain how governments responded to economic crises in the interwar period.
Explain causes and consequences of World War II.
Explain causes and consequences of mass atrocities 1900-present.
common_question_types: Analyze a WWI-era primary source (propaganda poster, political cartoon, speech) for purpose and audience.
Compare causes of WWI and WWII.
Explain how the Treaty of Versailles contributed to the rise of fascism.
Identify similarities and differences in how governments used propaganda in both world wars.
exam_tips: Total war means the mobilization of entire civilian economies and populations — not just military forces.
The Treaty of Versailles is a cause of WWII: connect German resentment of reparations and territorial losses to the rise of Hitler.
Mass atrocities questions require you to identify both the specific event AND its broader historical context (nationalist ideology, state collapse, etc.).

units.name: Cold War and Decolonization
key_topics: Cold War structure: US vs. USSR, NATO vs. Warsaw Pact, containment, domino theory
Proxy wars: Korean War, Vietnam War
Non-Aligned Movement: Nasser, Sukarno, Kwame Nkrumah
China: Mao Zedong, Great Leap Forward, Cultural Revolution
Decolonization: negotiated vs. armed independence movements
Key independence leaders: Gandhi (India), Ho Chi Minh (Vietnam), Nasser (Egypt), Mandela (South Africa)
Partition of India (1947): Hindu India and Muslim Pakistan, 14 million displaced
Apartheid and anti-apartheid movement in South Africa
End of Cold War: Glasnost, Perestroika, Soviet collapse
learning_objectives: Explain the historical context of the Cold War and the ideological struggle between capitalism and communism.
Compare how the US and USSR maintained global influence through alliances, aid, and proxy conflicts.
Explain causes and consequences of China's adoption of communism.
Compare the processes by which peoples pursued independence — negotiated vs. armed struggle.
Explain how political changes led to territorial, demographic, and nationalist developments.
Explain the extent to which the Cold War produced change in the second half of the 20th century.
common_question_types: Analyze how the Non-Aligned Movement challenged Cold War bipolarity.
Compare negotiated independence (India) with armed independence struggles (Vietnam, Algeria).
Explain how Cold War competition shaped the policies of newly independent states.
Identify causes and consequences of the Partition of India.
exam_tips: The Non-Aligned Movement is a key counternarrative — newly independent nations often rejected both US capitalism and Soviet communism.
Great Leap Forward and Cultural Revolution both caused massive human suffering — know approximate death tolls and causes.
Glasnost (openness) and Perestroika (restructuring) were Gorbachev's reforms that inadvertently accelerated Soviet collapse.

units.name: Globalization
key_topics: Technological advances: radio, internet, air travel, Green Revolution, birth control, vaccines, nuclear energy
Disease and population: 1918 flu pandemic, HIV/AIDS, Ebola, malaria, increased longevity
Environmental changes: deforestation, climate change, fossil fuel dependence
Global economic institutions: WTO, World Bank, IMF, NAFTA, ASEAN, European Union
Neoliberalism: Reagan, Thatcher, Deng Xiaoping's market reforms
Multinational corporations (MNCs) and knowledge economy
Cultural globalization: Bollywood, global consumer brands, spread of Western culture
Resistance to globalization: anti-IMF activism, local cultural movements
Human rights and reform: Universal Declaration of Human Rights, feminism, caste reservation, end of apartheid, Greenpeace
learning_objectives: Explain how new technologies changed the world and human populations 1900-present.
Explain causes and effects of environmental changes in the 20th-21st centuries.
Explain continuities and changes in the global economy — free markets, trading blocs, MNCs.
Explain how social categories and practices were challenged through reform movements.
Explain how globalization changed culture and identity.
Explain responses to globalization, including resistance movements.
common_question_types: Analyze how economic globalization has produced both integration and resistance.
Explain the environmental consequences of 20th-century industrialization and technology.
Compare cultural globalization's effects across different world regions.
Explain the role of international organizations (UN, WTO, IMF) in shaping the global economy.
exam_tips: Globalization produces winners and losers — the exam often asks about both benefits (economic growth, technology spread) and drawbacks (inequality, cultural homogenization).
Know specific international organizations and their functions: WTO (trade rules), World Bank (development loans), IMF (financial stability), UN (peacekeeping and human rights).
The Green Revolution increased agricultural yields but also created environmental and economic dependency concerns — this is a common SAQ or LEQ topic.
